questions.to_find: Number of times the process enters the ready queue (excluding initial entry)

understand.plain: A C program runs a loop 4 times. Each iteration calls scanf (an input I/O) and printf (an output I/O). Whenever a process does I/O it goes to the I/O queue, and a context switch happens. When the I/O finishes, the process returns to the ready queue (another context switch). We need to count how many times it re-enters the ready queue.

keywords.explain: A queue where a process waits while it is blocked on an I/O operation (e.g., reading from keyboard or writing to screen).
keywords.example: When scanf is called, the process is moved to the I/O queue until the user types input.

keywords.explain: The CPU switches from one process to another, saving and restoring state. It happens here both when a process requests I/O and when it returns from I/O.
keywords.example: After printf completes its I/O, the process re-enters the ready queue triggering a context switch.

keywords.explain: A queue of processes that are ready to run and are waiting for the CPU.
keywords.example: After returning from I/O, the process joins the ready queue before getting CPU time again.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 160' width='520' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='55' width='100' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Running</text><rect x='200' y='10' width='100' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>I/O Queue</text><rect x='200' y='100' width='100' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Ready Queue</text><line x1='110' y1='65' x2='200' y2='30' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='145' y='45' class='sub mut'>I/O request</text><line x1='300' y1='30' x2='390' y2='60' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='330' y='55' class='sub mut'>I/O done</text><line x1='390' y1='80' x2='300' y2='110' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr2)'/><text x='320' y='105' class='sub ink'>enters</text><text x='315' y='120' class='sub ink'>ready Q</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker><marker id='arr2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#1D9E75'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: State diagram: process moves from Running to I/O Queue on I/O request, then to Ready Queue when I/O completes, and back to Running when scheduled.

given.aim: Count how many times the process re-enters the ready queue during its lifetime (excluding initial entry).

terms.example: 4 iterations means 4 scanf calls and 4 printf calls.
terms.connects: Each I/O call causes the process to leave and then re-enter the ready queue once.

terms.meaning: Each iteration has 2 I/O calls: scanf (input) and printf (output).
terms.example: Iteration i=0: scanf triggers I/O, then printf triggers I/O.
terms.connects: 2 I/O per iteration * 4 iterations = 8 total I/O operations.

terms.term: Re-entry to ready queue per I/O
terms.meaning: After each I/O completes, the process enters the ready queue once (context switch on return from I/O).
terms.example: After scanf completes, process enters ready queue: 1 re-entry.
terms.connects: 8 I/O operations => 8 times re-entering the ready queue.

given.plan: Count total I/O operations (scanf and printf each count as one I/O). For each I/O, the process re-enters the ready queue once when the I/O finishes. The initial entry into the ready queue when the process starts is not counted.

steps.title: Count I/O operations
steps.apply: The loop runs 4 times. Each iteration has: scanf (1 I/O) + printf (1 I/O) = 2 I/O operations per iteration. Total I/O = 4 * 2 = 8.
steps.note: Both scanf and printf are system calls that involve I/O and block the process.

steps.title: Count ready queue entries
steps.formula_raw: Ready queue entries = Total I/O (one re-entry per I/O completion)
steps.apply: Each time the process requests I/O it is moved to the I/O queue. Each time I/O completes, the process re-enters the ready queue. So: 8 I/O completions => 8 re-entries into the ready queue. The question says NOT to count the initial entry when the process first starts.
steps.note: Context switch happens both when I/O is requested (process leaves CPU) and when I/O returns (process enters ready queue). Only the 'return from I/O' events count as entries into the ready queue here.

solution.result: The process enters the ready queue 8 times (excluding the initial entry).

questions.formula_note: Each I/O operation causes one re-entry into the ready queue upon I/O completion. Total ready queue re-entries = total number of I/O system calls executed.

questions.question:
I/O redirection
A. implies changing the name of a file
B. can be employed to use an existing file as input file for a program
C. implies connecting programs through a pipe
D. None of the above
questions.answer: B. can be employed to use an existing file as input file for a program
questions.to_find: The correct statement describing I/O redirection

understand.plain: I/O redirection is a shell feature that allows the standard input or output of a program to be redirected from/to a file instead of the keyboard/screen. The question asks which of the given statements correctly describes this concept.

keywords.explain: A mechanism in operating systems (shells) that allows standard input (stdin) or standard output (stdout) to be directed to/from files rather than the default keyboard/terminal.
keywords.example: The command 'prog < input.txt' redirects stdin so the program reads from input.txt instead of the keyboard.

keywords.explain: A mechanism that connects the output of one program to the input of another program; different from I/O redirection to/from files.
keywords.example: 'ls | grep .txt' connects ls output to grep input via a pipe.

understand.visual_alt: I/O redirection: a program's stdin is connected to a file (< file) or stdout to a file (> file), as opposed to the keyboard or screen.

given.aim: Identify the correct description of I/O redirection.

terms.term: Option A: changing the name of a file
terms.meaning: Renaming a file — this is done with the 'rename' or 'mv' command, not I/O redirection.
terms.example: 'mv old.txt new.txt' renames a file, unrelated to I/O redirection.
terms.connects: Incorrect — I/O redirection does not rename files.

terms.term: Option B: use an existing file as input
terms.meaning: Using '<' operator to redirect stdin to read from a file, so the program reads from that file instead of the keyboard.
terms.example: 'sort < data.txt' uses data.txt as input to the sort program.
terms.connects: Correct — this is exactly what I/O redirection (specifically input redirection) does.

terms.term: Option C: connecting programs through a pipe
terms.meaning: Pipes (|) connect programs, which is a related but distinct IPC mechanism from I/O redirection.
terms.example: 'ls | more' is a pipe, not I/O redirection.
terms.connects: Incorrect — pipes are different from I/O redirection.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the definition of I/O redirection. I/O redirection connects a program's standard I/O to files; pipes connect programs to each other.

steps.title: Define I/O redirection
steps.apply: I/O redirection allows the standard input or output of a program to be read from or written to a file. For example, 'prog < infile.txt' causes the program to read from infile.txt instead of the keyboard.
steps.note: This is a shell-level feature available in Unix/Linux and Windows command shells.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A: Changing a file's name — this is file renaming (mv/rename), not I/O redirection. WRONG.
B: Using an existing file as input — this is precisely input redirection ('< file'). CORRECT.
C: Connecting programs through a pipe — this is piping (|), a separate IPC mechanism. WRONG.
D: None of the above — eliminated since B is correct.
steps.note: Pipes and I/O redirection are often confused, but pipes connect processes while redirection connects processes to files.

solution.result: Option B is correct: I/O redirection can be employed to use an existing file as the input file for a program.

questions.formula_note: I/O redirection (< for input, > for output) connects a process's standard I/O to files; pipes (|) connect one process's output to another process's input — these are distinct OS mechanisms.

questions.question:
The details of an interrupt cycle are shown in figure. Given that an interrupt input arrives every 1 msec, what is the percentage of the total time that the CPU devotes for the main program execution?

[Figure: Interrupt cycle timing diagram showing:
- Interrupt latency: 0.1 msec (time from interrupt arrival to start of ISR)
- Interrupt service time (ISR execution): 0.1 msec
- Total interrupt overhead per cycle: 0.2 msec
- Interrupt period: 1 msec]

(Answer as a percentage)
questions.to_find: Percentage of CPU time devoted to main program execution

understand.plain: An interrupt arrives every 1 millisecond. Each interrupt takes some time to service (the Interrupt Service Routine, ISR). The remaining time in each 1 msec period is available for the main program. We need to find what fraction of the 1 msec period is spent on the main program.

keywords.explain: The time delay from when an interrupt signal arrives until the CPU actually starts executing the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
keywords.example: If interrupt latency is 0.1 msec, the CPU takes 0.1 msec after the interrupt signal to begin the ISR.

keywords.explain: The piece of code that the CPU executes in response to an interrupt. After the ISR completes, the CPU returns to the interrupted program.
keywords.example: A keyboard interrupt ISR reads the pressed key and stores it in a buffer.

keywords.explain: The total CPU time consumed per interrupt, including latency, ISR execution, and return overhead. This time is not available for the main program.
keywords.example: If interrupt overhead = 0.2 msec per interrupt and interrupts arrive every 1 msec, overhead = 20% of CPU time.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 130' width='500' height='130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='30' width='320' height='35' rx='4' class='ok' opacity='0.7'/><text x='170' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Main Program (0.8 msec)</text><rect x='340' y='30' width='64' height='35' rx='4' class='no' opacity='0.8'/><text x='372' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>ISR (0.2 ms)</text><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='404' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='20' y1='75' x2='20' y2='85' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='404' y1='75' x2='404' y2='85' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='212' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1 msec (interrupt period)</text><line x1='340' y1='10' x2='340' y2='75' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='340' y='8' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>Interrupt</text><text x='372' y='15' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>0.2 msec</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing 1 msec interrupt period. The main program runs for 0.8 msec, then an interrupt occurs and the ISR runs for 0.2 msec, repeating every 1 msec.

given.aim: Find the percentage of CPU time spent executing the main program.

terms.meaning: The time between consecutive interrupts; T = 1 msec.
terms.example: An interrupt arrives at t=0, the next at t=1 msec, and so on.
terms.connects: This is the total cycle time; the main program runs for T minus the interrupt overhead.

terms.meaning: Total time spent handling one interrupt = interrupt latency + ISR execution time. From the figure: 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.2 msec.
terms.example: CPU spends 0.2 msec per millisecond servicing interrupts.
terms.connects: Overhead fraction = 0.2/1.0 = 20%, leaving 80% for main program.

terms.meaning: T - overhead = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8 msec per cycle is available for the main program.
terms.example: Out of every 1 msec, 0.8 msec goes to the main program.

given.plan: Calculate interrupt overhead per cycle from the figure (latency + ISR time). Subtract from the interrupt period to get main program time. Express as a percentage.

steps.title: Determine interrupt overhead per cycle
steps.apply: From the figure: interrupt latency = 0.1 msec, ISR execution time = 0.1 msec. Total overhead per interrupt = 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.2 msec.
steps.note: The overhead includes the time to save CPU state, execute the ISR, and restore CPU state before returning to the main program.

steps.title: Calculate percentage time for main program
steps.apply:
T = 1 msec (interrupt arrives every 1 msec).
Main program time = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8 msec.
% = (0.8 / 1.0) * 100 = 80%.
steps.note: This assumes the interrupt overhead is constant and the CPU is always busy (either running the main program or handling interrupts).

solution.result: The CPU devotes 80% of its total time to main program execution.

questions.formula_note: CPU efficiency for main program = (1 - interrupt_overhead/interrupt_period) * 100%. Here (1 - 0.2/1.0) * 100 = 80%.
